intro:
Your electricity bill is the sum of every device running in your home. Understanding how much each appliance costs to run helps you identify the biggest energy hogs, make smart purchasing decisions, and find meaningful ways to reduce your bill. Our appliance energy cost calculator shows daily, monthly, and annual running costs for any device based on its wattage and usage hours.

Energy cost is calculated as: Cost = Watts ÷ 1,000 × Hours × Days × kWh Rate. For example, a 1,500-watt space heater running 8 hours/day at $0.13/kWh costs about $3.12 per day and $93.60 per month. The US average electricity rate is about $0.13/kWh (check your bill for your local rate).

Common appliance wattages: refrigerator 100–400W, electric oven 2,000–5,000W, clothes dryer 4,000–6,000W, desktop computer 80–500W, LED bulb 8–12W, air conditioner (window unit) 900–1,440W.
howItWorks: Enter the appliance's wattage (found on the label or specification sheet), hours of use per day, days per month, and your electricity cost per kWh (from your utility bill). The calculator converts watts to kilowatts, multiplies by hours and days to get total kWh, then multiplies by rate for cost.
interpretationGuide: Monthly kWh shows how much electricity the appliance consumes. Multiply by 12 for annual consumption to understand its impact on your overall usage. Compare to your total monthly kWh usage from your electricity bill to see each appliance's share of total consumption.

faqs.question: Where do I find the wattage of my appliance?
faqs.answer: Check the label on the appliance itself (usually on the back, bottom, or inside the door). The label will show wattage (W) or amps (A) and voltage (V). If listed in amps: Watts = Amps × Voltage (usually 120V in the US). Also check the owner's manual or the manufacturer's website.

faqs.question: What is the average US electricity rate?
faqs.answer: The US national average is approximately $0.13–$0.16 per kWh (2024). Hawaii has the highest rates (~$0.34/kWh); Louisiana and Oklahoma have the lowest (~$0.09/kWh). Find your exact rate on your monthly electricity bill — it is typically listed as cents per kWh in the rate schedule section.

faqs.question: What are the most expensive appliances to run?
faqs.answer: Electric water heater: $40–$60/month. Central air conditioner: $50–$120/month (summer). Electric dryer: $20–$40/month. Electric oven: $3–$10/month. Space heater (1,500W, 8 hrs/day): $30–$50/month. Refrigerator: $8–$15/month. LED bulb (12W, 8 hrs): $0.50/month.

faqs.question: How do I calculate kWh from my appliance?
faqs.answer: kWh = Watts ÷ 1,000 × Hours. A 1,200-watt microwave running 30 minutes: 1,200 ÷ 1,000 × 0.5 = 0.6 kWh. At $0.13/kWh, that is 7.8 cents per use. Running it twice daily: 1.2 kWh/day = $0.156/day = ~$4.68/month.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to leave a TV on all day?
faqs.answer: A modern 55-inch LED TV uses 60–80 watts. At 80W, 8 hours/day, $0.13/kWh: 80 × 8 ÷ 1,000 × 0.13 = $0.083/day = $2.50/month = $30/year. Older plasma TVs used 300–500W — 5–6× more expensive to run.

faqs.question: Does unplugging devices save money?
faqs.answer: Yes, but modestly. Standby power ('phantom load' or 'vampire power') from devices left plugged in accounts for about 10% of average electricity bills. Common standby users: game consoles (10–15W), cable boxes (15–25W), desktop computers (sleep mode, 5–10W), phone chargers (0.1–0.5W when idle). Smart power strips help.

faqs.question: How can I reduce my electricity bill the most?
faqs.answer: Biggest impacts: switch to a newer ENERGY STAR refrigerator (saves $50–$150/yr). Upgrade HVAC (30–40% of your bill). Install a programmable thermostat. Replace electric water heater with heat pump water heater (saves 60–70%). Switch to LED lighting throughout. Seal air leaks (reduces heating/cooling by 10–20%).

faqs.question: How do I read my electricity bill in kWh?
faqs.answer: Your electricity bill shows total kWh used in the billing period. Divide by days in the period for daily average. Compare month-over-month to track changes. Most bills also show your cost per kWh in the rate summary — use this in the calculator for accurate results specific to your utility.

faqs.question: What is the ENERGY STAR label and does it matter?
faqs.answer: ENERGY STAR is a US EPA program certifying that appliances meet strict efficiency standards. ENERGY STAR refrigerators use at least 9–10% less energy than standard models; washers use 25% less water and energy; dishwashers use 12% less water. The premium is usually recovered in 2–4 years of energy savings.

faqs.question: How do solar panels affect the energy cost calculation?
faqs.answer: If you have solar panels, your effective cost per kWh depends on whether you are using solar-generated electricity (near zero cost) or grid electricity. Many solar customers track net metering — the calculator is useful for understanding consumption patterns even with solar, as high-consumption times may still draw from the grid.
